content.problemStatement:
Rafid is teaching his students plane geometry. Today they are talking about convex polygons. A convex polygon is a simple non-degenerate polygon such that each of its internal angles is strictly smaller than 180∘180^\circ180∘.

Rafid drew NNN points (numbered 111 through NNN) on a blackboard (a two-dimensional plane) and told each student to choose some of the points and connect them to obtain a convex polygon. For each valid iii, the coordinates of the iii-th point are (Xi,Yi)(X_i, Y_i)(Xi​,Yi​).

There are KKK students (numbered 111 through KKK). To prevent the students from choosing the same polygon over and over, Rafid told them that they have to choose polygons one by one in the order from student 111 to student KKK and each student has to choose a set of points which was not chosen by any earlier student. That means they have to choose KKK different sets of points, resulting in KKK different polygons. As a result, some students may end up unable to choose any polygon.

To encourage the students, Rafid announced that each student will receive a number of candies proportional to the area of this student's polygon. The students are very greedy, so the first student will choose the polygon with the largest area, the second student will choose the polygon with the largest area which does not use the same set of points as the first student's polygon, and so on.

For each student, help Rafid find the area of the polygon chosen by this student or determine that this student cannot choose a polygon anymore. Note that while there may be many ways in which the students could choose their polygons, their areas are uniquely determined.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers N and K. Then N lines follow, each containing two integers Xi and Yi – the coordinates of the i‑th point.
content.outputFormat: Print K space‑separated integers A1 … AK. Ai is twice the area of the polygon chosen by the i‑th student, or -1 if no new convex polygon exists for that student.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
The following figure describes the points and some polygons.


The first student chooses the largest polygon ABCD\mathsf{ABCD}ABCD with area 100100100.
The next four students choose the triangles ABC\mathsf{ABC}ABC, BCD\mathsf{BCD}BCD, CDA\mathsf{CDA}CDA and DAB\mathsf{DAB}DAB, each with area 505050. Note that for example, the polygon AECB\mathsf{AECB}AECB is not convex because it has one internal angle equal to 180∘180^{\circ}180∘.
The next four students choose the triangles AEB\mathsf{AEB}AEB, BEC\mathsf{BEC}BEC, CED\mathsf{CED}CED and DEA\mathsf{DEA}DEA, each with area 252525.
There are no more polygons the students can choose, so the remaining six students are left without a polygon.

solution.approach:
Every convex polygon is uniquely defined by the cyclic order of its vertices.  A set of points forms a convex polygon iff the points are in convex position – no chosen point lies inside the convex hull of the others.  The number of such subsets for N ≤ 120 is O(N^3) (a classic result).  We can enumerate all convex polygons in O(N^3) time using an angular‑sweep DP:
1. Sort all points by (x, y).  For each point p treat it as the lowest (lexicographically) vertex of a polygon – this guarantees that each polygon is generated exactly once.
2. For a fixed base point p, sort the remaining points by polar angle around p.
3. Let the sorted list be v1 … vm.  For every ordered pair (vi, vj) with i < j we keep a DP entry dp[vi][vj] – a list of areas of all convex chains that start at p, go to vi, then to vj and are strictly turning left at every step.
4. When we extend a chain (vi, vj) with a new vertex vk (j < k) we check the orientation of (vi, vj, vk).  If it is a left turn we can append vk, forming a new chain (vj, vk).  The area of the new chain is the old area plus the signed area of triangle (p, vj, vk).
5. Whenever a chain has length ≥ 3 we have a complete convex polygon (the chain together with the closing edge back to p).  Its total doubled area is the accumulated area plus the area of triangle (p, last, first).
6. All obtained doubled areas are stored in a global vector.
7. After processing every base point we have a list of the doubled areas of **all** convex polygons, each appearing exactly once.  Sort this list in descending order.
8. The i‑th student receives the i‑th element of the sorted list; if i exceeds the list size output -1.
The algorithm runs in O(N^3) time (the DP loops over ordered triples) and uses O(N^2) auxiliary memory for the DP tables.  The total number of convex polygons for N = 120 is at most a few million, well within memory limits, and sorting them is O(P log P) where P is the number of polygons (P ≤ O(N^3)).
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^3 + P log P) where P is the number of convex polygons (P = O(N^3)).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N^2 + P) – DP tables plus the list of all polygon areas.
